region_of_origin: Vietnam (Southern Vietnam); also widely served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese broken rice (cơm tấm). The broader 'rice dish' term (FOODON_03315671) is used as the closest defensible parent anchor. A more specific term for cơm tấm is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Vietnamese broken rice with pork and egg meatloaf. A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is used as the closest defensible parent category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Vietnamese dish. SNOMED CT does not provide food composition codes at this level of specificity for Southeast Asian dishes.

main_ingredients: egg meatloaf / steamed egg cake (chả trứng)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of cơm tấm sườn chả is approximately 400g, comprising roughly 200g broken rice, 100g grilled pork chop, 60g egg meatloaf, and 40g of garnishes including cucumber, tomato, and pickled vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Cơm Tấm Sườn Chả — Broken Rice with Grilled Pork Chop and Egg Meatloaf
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cơm Tấm Sườn Chả (cr_id G00010556). All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is set to -1 (data not available). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and detailed vitamin/mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice (boiled, high-GI variety)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese broken rice (tấm) as a composite dish with pork chop and egg meatloaf. Broken rice is a fragmented form of white rice with a similar starch structure and comparable GI to white rice. GI of 72 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled white rice (GI ~72), which is the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (72 × 63.75) / 100 = 45.9, based on the effective carbohydrate contribution of the rice component (~63.75g carbohydrate from rice in a 400g serving), accounting for the mixed-dish effect where protein and fat from pork chop and egg meatloaf moderate the overall glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g (borderline range); partially offset by potassium 327mg/100g and dietary fibre 3.7g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231mg/100g is in the borderline range for hypertension; patients should request fish sauce dipping sauce on the side and use sparingly, avoid pickled vegetables or request reduced quantity, and be aware that the grilled pork chop marinade (fish sauce, soy sauce) contributes additional sodium — a 400g serving delivers approximately 924mg sodium before condiments, approaching 40% of the 2300mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI broken rice (GI ~72) contributing 25.2g carbohydrate/100g with a high glycemic load per serving (~45.9 GL units)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Broken rice has a high glycemic index (~72) comparable to white rice, and a standard 400g serving delivers approximately 101g total carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~45.9; patients with type 2 diabetes should significantly reduce portion size, request extra non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre and slow glucose absorption, avoid the fish sauce dipping sauce which contains added sugar, and consider substituting with a smaller rice portion paired with additional protein.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (4.21g/100g) from grilled pork chop; saturated fat data unavailable; sodium 231mg/100g; iron 2.55mg/100g is beneficial
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The grilled pork chop contributes saturated fat (exact value not reported in HPB source) and dietary cholesterol; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size of the pork chop, request reduced marinade to lower sodium intake, and note that the dish provides beneficial potassium (327mg/100g) and iron (2.55mg/100g) which partially offset cardiovascular risk factors — overall the dish is acceptable in moderation with portion control and condiment reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork chop is red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6); high-GI rice promotes pro-inflammatory glycemic load; tomatoes are nightshades
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of this dish due to the pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acid profile of pork (arachidonic acid amplifies inflammatory cytokine production), the high-GI broken rice base which promotes inflammatory mediators, and the presence of tomatoes which are nightshades that may trigger individual RA flares — request extra cucumber and non-nightshade vegetables to increase anti-inflammatory fibre content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cơm Tấm Sườn Chả (Broken Rice with Grilled Pork Chop and Egg Meatloaf) is a quintessential Southern Vietnamese rice dish that has become one of the most popular street foods and casual restaurant meals in Vietnam and among Vietnamese communities in Singapore. The dish centres on cơm tấm — broken rice grains (tấm), which are the fragmented by-products of rice milling that were historically consumed by poorer communities but are now prized for their slightly firmer, more textured bite compared to whole-grain white rice. The broken rice is steamed and served as the base of the plate. The primary protein component is sườn — a thin, marinated pork chop (typically loin or rib cut) that is marinated in a mixture of fish sauce, lemongrass, garlic, shallots, sugar, and sometimes soy sauce, then grilled over charcoal or a gas grill until caramelised and slightly charred at the edges, delivering a smoky, savoury-sweet flavour. The second protein component is chả — a Vietnamese steamed egg meatloaf (also called chả trứng) made from ground pork, wood ear mushrooms, glass noodles, and beaten eggs, steamed in a mould until set into a firm, golden-topped cake with a soft, savoury interior. The plate is garnished with sliced cucumber, tomato, spring onion in oil, and often pickled daikon and carrot (đồ chua). A side of nước chấm (fish sauce dipping sauce sweetened with sugar and lime juice) is served separately. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, 231mg sodium, 327mg potassium, 54mg calcium, 40mg magnesium, and 2.55mg iron. The broken rice base has a high glycemic index (~72), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding pork. It is commonly served at lunch and dinner and is a culturally significant comfort food in Vietnamese cuisine.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010556, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records. The dish is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved GI source (University of Sydney GI Database, boiled white rice GI 72) with clear documentation of the analogous estimation basis.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (G00010556) as of 2026-04-16. Detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data are not available in the HPB source for this composite Vietnamese dish. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source. This is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010556); the egg meatloaf and pork chop components would be expected to contribute some cholesterol in a typical preparation, and this value may reflect a measurement artefact or rounding in the HPB record — downstream users should treat this value with caution and may wish to cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central values for similar composite dishes. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for cơm tấm as a composite dish is available in approved sources. 4. This is a Vietnamese dish served in Singapore. Per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 7.2, Vietnamese is the mandatory native-script language for Vietnamese dishes. The dish is also served in Singapore (region_tags: SG), so Malay, Tamil, and Hindi descriptive translations have been added to local_names and aliases to satisfy the Singapore multilingual context. These are descriptive translations rather than established local names, as the dish is not a native Singaporean dish. 5. The dish is not certified halal as the preparation typically involves pork. 6. ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type are set to 'closest_parent' as no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 term exists for this specific Vietnamese composite dish.
